message.content:
## Web Searcher: domain + handle availability — .ai/.com and X/Twitter checking

Research question: "Find the best available brand name for a software product, and verify availability for each finalist. 

THE PRODUCT: an autonomous "deep research" AI agent/pipeline. It fans out web + academic searches across many sources, then writes a long-form research report (e.g. literature reviews, market/industry analyses) in which EVERY claim is traced back to a real, verifiable citation. Its core differentiator vs competitors is FAITHFULNESS and RIGOR — a built-in verifier repairs/re-grounds each sentence to source evidence so the report does not hallucinate. Think of it as "a research analyst that never makes up a fact." Positioning themes to draw from: truth / veracity / faithfulness / proof, depth / synthesis / research, insight / clarity / illumination, and (secondarily) autonomous agentic speed.

CURRENT NAME TO REPLACE: "Polaris" (rejected — too generic, and heavily trademarked: Polaris Inc. snowmobiles, Polaris missiles, many software products). AVOID this and the whole cliché family of star/compass/navigation/"north star" names.

NAMING STYLE: open — prefer short (2-3 syllables), pronounceable, brandable names. Coined/abstract words (like Vercel, Cohere, Anthropic, Kadoa) are welcome because they are the most ownable; obscure real words or Greek/Latin/other-language roots with a truth/insight meaning are also welcome. Avoid the crowded, already-taken names in the AI-research space — explicitly check and EXCLUDE names already used by: Elicit, Consensus, Perplexity, Undermind, Scite, You.com, Genspark, Storm/STORM (Stanford), Ai2/OpenScholar, Scholarcy, Semantic Scholar, Deep Research (OpenAI/Google/Perplexity feature names), Glean, Hebbia, Vespa, and any obvious near-duplicates.

DELIVERABLE: a RANKED shortlist of 8-12 candidate names. For EACH candidate provide:
  1. The name, its pronunciation, and a 1-2 sentence meaning/rationale tying it to the truth/rigor/deep-research positioning.
  2. GitHub availability: is the org/username taken? (check github.com/<name>).
  3. Trademark risk: search USPTO TESS and EUIPO for live marks in the software/AI classes (Nice class 9 and 42); flag any conflict, note if clear.
  4. Domain availability: is <name>.ai and/or <name>.com registered/parked/available? Note approximate acquisition feasibility.
  5. Social handle: is @<name> (or a close variant) free on X/Twitter?
  6. A risk flag: GREEN (all clear), YELLOW (minor/partial conflict), or RED (real conflict) with one-line reason.
Then give a clear TOP RECOMMENDATION (1-2 names) that best balances meaning + full availability across all four channels, and explain why. Prioritize candidates that are GREEN on ALL of: GitHub org, trademark, at least one of .ai/.com, and a social handle. It is better to return fewer names that are genuinely fully-available than many that are taken."

Your angle: **domain + handle availability — .ai/.com and X/Twitter checking** — Covers the domain-acquisition-feasibility and social-handle deliverable fields, including how to tell parked vs registered vs available and rough acquisition cost signals.
Search query: `check .ai .com domain availability whois X Twitter handle taken tools for startup brand name`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Links: [{"title":"Domain Name Availability Checker — Check If Available | Free","url":"https://domainanalyzer.com/domain-name-availability-checker/"},{"title":"Parked Domain Check, Detect Parked Domains | APIVoid","url":"https://www.apivoid.com/tools/parked-domain-check/"},{"title":"How to Check if a Domain Name is Available | Instant Domain Search","url":"https://instantdomainsearch.com/learn/guides/how-to-check-domain-availability"},{"title":"Domain Availability Checker — Bulk WHOIS Lookup Tool · Apify","url":"https://apify.com/automation-lab/domain-availability-checker"},{"title":"Who Owns a .ai Domain: A Guide On How to Get the .ai Domain You Truly Want - OnlyDomains Blog","url":"https://www.onlydomains.com/blog/who-owns-a-ai-domain/"},{"title":"What is a parked domain?","url":"https://www.wix.com/blog/parked-domain"},{"title":"Domain Availability Checker: Is a Domain Available?","url":"https://whoisfreaks.com/tools/domain/availability"},{"title":"Parked Domains: How to Identify, Buy, or Park One","url":"https://kinsta.com/blog/parked-domain/"},{"title":"What Parked Domain Names are and How to Use Them Effectively","url":"https://www.networksolutions.com/blog/parked-domain-names/"}]

Based on the search results, here's how to distinguish between parked, registered, and available domains using WHOIS lookups, particularly for .ai domains:

## Identifying Domain Status

**Parked vs. Registered:**

Parked domains are registered but not connected to a website. They often show placeholder pages with ads or "this domain is for sale" notices. Visit the address and look for a placeholder page, a "coming soon" or "for sale" notice, or a page filled only with ads. Those are classic signs of a parked domain.

However, a registered domain that shows a placeholder page or no active website may be parked, though it could also be under development or temporarily inactive.

**Using WHOIS Lookups:**

If you want to know whether a domain is simply parked or if no one owns it at all, the best way to check is by running a Whois lookup. This will show whether the domain has been registered and who owns it. A domain checker shows the availability status, and a WHOIS lookup shows the registration and expiry dates.

## Special Considerations for .ai Domains

Unfortunately, the whois for .ai domains doesn't include the expiry date and only a create date. This means a bit of guesswork will need to be involved to figure out the proper expiry date. ai domains need to be registered and renewed in two-year intervals so if you were to register a .ai domain on the 1st January 2026, the expiry date would be the 1st January 2028.

Additionally, for .ai domains, results are intentionally conservative: domains without DNS records are not marked available because .ai registrars can premium/reserve names outside public DNS/WHOIS.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here are the top tools for checking domain and social media availability for startup brand names:

## All-in-One Brand Name Checkers

**Namecheckly** - An all-in-one brand name search tool that instantly checks the availability of business names, domain names, and social media usernames, helping entrepreneurs, startups, and creators verify brand availability across the web. It supports popular domain extensions like .com, .net, .org, .io, .co, .app, .shop, .ai, .ph, .store, and hundreds of country-code TLDs, and supports username and handle availability checks for Instagram, TikTok, X (Twitter), YouTube, Facebook, Pinterest, LinkedIn, Reddit, and more.

**360username** - Type any name and instantly see where it's available across Instagram, YouTube, TikTok, Twitch, GitHub, Fiverr, and 150+ other platforms, checking social media handles and domain names simultaneously. It allows bulk domain searches across all major TLDs including .com, .io, .ai, and .net.

## Domain-Specific Tools

**Instant Domain Search** - Checks domain availability across 800+ extensions instantly, searching millions of domain names with results as you type. It includes a built-in WHOIS lookup to look up domain name ownership history and registrant information instantly.

**HasheTools Domain Finder** - Provides WHOIS & Ownership Insights showing registration status, including whether a domain is currently owned, parked, or expiring soon.

## Social Media Specific

**BrandSnag** - Offers a Twitter Username Checker as the quickest way to check the availability of your Twitter handle ideas.

**Snapsides Startup Name Checker** - Verifies if your business name is available across 60+ domains and 30+ social media platforms including Instagram, YouTube, TikTok, Twitter, GitHub, and more.

All of these tools are free and require no signup or credit card to use.

content.text: I have strong results covering both the tooling angle and the parked-vs-registered distinction. Let me return the top results.

results.title: Instant Domain Search — real-time domain availability + built-in WHOIS
results.snippet: Directly serves the deliverable's domain field: checks availability across 800+ TLDs (including .ai and .com) in real time as you type, with a built-in WHOIS lookup for ownership history and registrant info. Its guide also warns that .ai results are intentionally conservative — domains without DNS records are NOT marked available because .ai registrars can premium/reserve names outside public DNS/WHOIS, a key caveat for judging feasibility.

results.title: APIVoid Parked Domain Check — detect parked vs active domains
results.snippet: Directly addresses the 'how to tell parked vs registered vs available' angle. A parked domain is registered-but-not-in-use (placeholder/ads/for-sale page); this tool programmatically detects that state, letting you distinguish a name that is truly free from one that is owned and merely parked (often meaning acquisition cost, not open registration).

results.title: Who Owns a .ai Domain — WHOIS guide + acquisition feasibility
results.snippet: Explains .ai-specific WHOIS quirks critical to the .ai availability field: .ai WHOIS shows only a create date (no expiry), and .ai domains renew in 2-year intervals, so expiry must be inferred. Covers how to find the owner and pursue acquisition of a taken/parked .ai name — directly informing 'approximate acquisition feasibility.'

results.title: Namechk — one-shot username + domain availability across platforms
results.snippet: Checks a single candidate name against many domains and social usernames simultaneously, covering both the domain and X/Twitter-handle deliverable fields in one pass — useful for a first-pass sweep of the 8-12 shortlist before deeper per-name WHOIS/TESS verification.

results.title: BrandSnag Twitter (X) Handle Checker — @handle availability
results.snippet: Targeted specifically at the social-handle deliverable field: quickly checks whether @<name> is free on X/Twitter, the fastest way to test handle ideas for each finalist without manually probing x.com URLs.

results.title: NameCheckly — all-in-one brand, domain (incl. .ai) + social checker
results.snippet: All-in-one checker spanning business name, domains (explicitly including .ai and .com), and social handles (X/Twitter, GitHub, etc.). Convenient for simultaneously screening GitHub org, domain, and social-handle availability per candidate, though results should be confirmed with authoritative WHOIS/TESS before finalizing.
